network.overall_strategy: The network centers on the best-supported prebiotic routes to threonine from the literature: (i) cyanosulfidic photoredox production of aldehydes from HCN followed by Strecker synthesis through lactaldehyde and through glyceraldehyde, (ii) Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to a hydantoin and then threonine, (iii) empirically observed spark-discharge production of the same precursor pool, and (iv) more weakly supported but literature-discussed routes via glycine-acetaldehyde aldol chemistry and reductive amination of the threonine keto-acid precursor. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks and concentration environments but avoids direct high-temperature threonine formation because of threonine fragility; surface UV/mineral chemistry performs the main target-forming steps; biochemical/prebiotic nodes capture threonine rapidly into peptides. Ten distinct pathways are defined as different valid traversals through the connected bipartite graph, while speculative steps are explicitly labeled as such and justified only where needed to complete the literature-grounded network.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; pH 9-11; anoxic; H2-rich micropores

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields formate as an early fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: This hydrothermal feeder step follows alkaline vent Fe-S chemistry where mackinawite and greigite precipitate and promote reduced-carbon formation; it is included to connect hydrothermal carbon fixation to downstream surface networks while avoiding direct high-temperature threonine synthesis because threonine is thermally fragile (White et al., 2015; Lane and Martin, 2012; Shock and Canovas, 1998).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system on sulfide-rich surface)

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox network with H2S reductant; UV irradiation; phosphate-buffered surface pools
conditions.other: UV-exposed cyanosulfidic chemistry in shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: UV photoredox cycling of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S reductant drives reductive homologation of HCN to a network of simple aldehydes including formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central literature-supported entry into threonine-relevant surface chemistry. Ritson and Sutherland experimentally established photoredox conversion of HCN through Cu(I) cyanide/H2S chemistry to formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. integrated this into a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism that also accesses glyceraldehyde and amino acid/nucleotide precursors (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). TiO2 is included as a plausible UV-active mineral surface in surface settings, though the key chemistry is the Cu(I) cyanide photoredox network.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system; UV light
conditions.other: 2 h irradiation; lactaldehyde obtained from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction; reported 19% yield

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, then undergoes photoreduction under cyanosulfidic conditions to produce lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland directly demonstrated that acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is photoreduced to lactaldehyde under Cu(I) cyanide/H2S/UV conditions, giving the key aldehyde precursor for threonine Strecker synthesis (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013). This is the most specific experimentally demonstrated entry to threonine chemistry.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted concentration on clay; aqueous Strecker conditions
conditions.other: imine formation with NH3 followed by cyanide addition; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium, then cyanide addition yields 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker route to the threonine aminonitrile is explicitly described in the deep research summary and rooted in the Ritson-Sutherland cyanosulfidic network; Patel et al. further identified this aminonitrile as an inevitable by-product of the same chemistry that generates nucleotide precursors (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). Montmorillonite is included as a plausible concentrating surface rather than as the primary mechanistic driver.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized glyceraldehyde in aqueous Strecker chemistry
conditions.other: anoxic shallow-pool setting; connects sugar and amino acid precursor chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Borate-stabilized glyceraldehyde undergoes imine formation with ammonia and cyanide addition to yield the same threonine aminonitrile hub.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified glyceraldehyde as a key shared branch point between ribonucleotide and amino acid precursor synthesis, and the research report specifically notes that glyceraldehyde plus NH3 plus HCN provides a parallel route to the threonine aminonitrile (Patel et al., 2015). Borate minerals are included because they stabilize sugars and improve surface plausibility.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: gives racemic threonine/allothreonine mixture; represented here as threonine node for target tracking

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the nitrile to the amino acid carboxylate under aqueous prebiotic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker aminonitrile-to-amino-acid conversion is the final step in the literature-supported threonine route. The deep research explicitly notes hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile to threonine and allothreonine as a racemic mixture of diastereomers, highlighting stereochemical nonselectivity (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Schroeder and Bada, 1977).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide and ammonium/carbonate equivalents to form a hydantoin ring, sequestering the amino acid precursor in a more stable intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies the Bucherer-Bergs route as a thermodynamically favorable alternative to direct Strecker chemistry and specifically proposes 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the threonine intermediate from lactaldehyde; this is supported by the general prebiotic hydantoin chemistry demonstrated by Pulletikurti et al. and meteoritic hydantoin occurrence (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner and Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: prebiotic hydrolysis of hydantoin reservoir to amino acid

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin yields the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin chemistry for amino acid synthesis from keto acids; the deep research extends the same logic to the threonine case via 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the specific threonine precursor (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge synthesis of HCN, aldehydes, and glycine from simple gases

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge; hot gas then aqueous trapping
conditions.other: modeled on CH4/N2/H2O with traces NH3 and CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S experiments; racemic products in complex mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reduced atmospheric gas mixtures generates HCN, NH3-derived products, aldehydes including acetaldehyde/lactaldehyde, and amino acids such as glycine in a complex product pool.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. confirmed threonine/allothreonine in spark discharge products from CH4/N2/H2O with traces NH3, while Parker et al. found threonine in archived H2S-containing Miller samples and Johnson et al. showed volcanic discharge settings enrich hydroxylated compounds. This node is intentionally framed as a precursor-pool source rather than a clean direct threonine reaction, addressing prior overcompression issues (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Conceptual aldol condensation of glycine with acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable; low-confidence conceptual route

reactions.mechanism: Reverse of threonine aldol cleavage: carbon-carbon bond formation between glycine equivalent and acetaldehyde yields threonine/allothreonine framework.
reactions.reasoning: This route is explicitly discussed in the deep research as conceptually simple but thermodynamically unfavorable and not demonstrated under purely prebiotic conditions. It is retained only as a literature-mentioned, low-confidence branch and not weighted equally with the Strecker routes (Fesko, 2016; Koga and Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Speculative formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde-derived carbonyl chemistry

conditions.other: network-bridging step; chemically plausible but not directly established in the supplied threonine literature

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-like carbon skeleton assembly and oxidation-state adjustment generate the alpha-keto-beta-hydroxy acid precursor to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The research report identifies 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as the keto-acid precursor to threonine but does not provide an experimentally demonstrated prebiotic synthesis of that specific keto acid. This step is therefore explicitly speculative and included only to connect the literature-supported reductive amination principle to the broader network (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: pH 8 for Ni/H2 conditions; extrapolated specifically to threonine precursor

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid undergoes reductive amination to the corresponding amino acid, preserving the beta-hydroxyl substituent.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is established prebiotically with Fe2+ at 70C and with H2/Ni at 22C, pH 8, but the threonine-specific precursor was not directly tested. The reaction is therefore a literature-grounded extrapolation rather than a demonstrated threonine synthesis (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis yielding heterogeneous amino acid/nucleobase mixture

conditions.catalyst: UV exposure and/or prolonged aqueous aging/freezing
conditions.other: nonselective polymerization-hydrolysis network; background source only

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes to complex nitrile polymers and heterocycles that upon hydrolysis release broad mixtures of amino acids and nucleobases.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research notes that HCN polymer hydrolysates can contain threonine among 12 proteinogenic amino acids, but this route is highly nonselective and chemically unresolved. It is therefore represented conservatively as a heterogeneous source process rather than a target-directed threonine reaction (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: repeated dehydration-rehydration cycles in geothermal pools to reduce free-threonine residence time

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation during wet-dry cycles forms a peptide bond, sequestering labile threonine into a more persistent oligomeric context.
reactions.reasoning: Threonine is unusually unstable in aqueous prebiotic settings, so rapid capture into peptides is a plausible use-before-decomposition strategy. Wet-dry cycling and mineral-assisted oligomerization are well supported for amino acid condensation, even though threonine-specific peptide yields are not reported in the deep research (Lahav et al., 1978; Bujdák et al., 1995; Kitadai et al., 2015; Damer and Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which is converted to lactaldehyde and then to threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker synthesis to the threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde
pathways.description: The lactaldehyde generated in cyanosulfidic chemistry is trapped as a hydantoin and later hydrolyzed to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to lactaldehyde Strecker completion
pathways.description: Electrical discharge supplies HCN and lactaldehyde-containing precursor pools; lactaldehyde then proceeds through Strecker chemistry to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to Bucherer-Bergs completion
pathways.description: Spark discharge produces lactaldehyde-containing mixtures that can enter the hydantoin route to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge precursor pool to aldol route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge produces glycine and acetaldehyde, which can in principle combine through the literature-discussed reverse aldol relationship to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer background route
pathways.description: Nonselective HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis yields threonine in heterogeneous product mixtures.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation feeds the connected network, after which surface cyanosulfidic chemistry makes aldehydes and the canonical lactaldehyde Strecker route yields threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon enters the integrated network, then surface cyanosulfidic chemistry generates glyceraldehyde, leading through the Strecker aminonitrile to threonine.

pathways.name: Integrated keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic aldehydes are funneled into the threonine keto-acid precursor, which then undergoes literature-grounded but threonine-specific extrapolated reductive amination to threonine.
